title: Replacing a toilet seat
subtitle: Remove an old toilet seat and fit a new one with correctly sized hinges
excerpt: How to remove the old fixings on a toilet seat that has worked loose or cracked, measure for the right replacement, and fit the new seat squarely. Covers top-fix and bottom-fix hinges, sticky bolts, and getting the seat to sit central on the pan.

glossaryTerms.definition: A toilet seat with hinges that fit from above through the holes in the pan. The bolts have a quick-release cap on top and a fixing nut below the pan rim. The standard modern replacement format.

An older toilet seat format where the hinge bolts pass through the pan from below and fix with a wing nut underneath. Requires the bolts to be threaded up through the holes; harder to fit when access under the pan is tight.

content.text: A toilet seat lasts five to ten years before the hinges loosen or the lid cracks. The job is short and needs almost no tools. The only choice point is whether the pan takes a

Most modern seats lift the hinge cap and release the seat with a press-and-slide on the hinge block. Lift the cap with a small flat screwdriver. If the seat does not slide off, the hinge bolts need to be undone. Reach under the pan rim and feel for the nut. Hold the nut with the spanner. Turn the bolt head from above with the flat screwdriver.

Measure across the pan rim between the two fixing holes. The standard is 150 mm. Measure the length of the pan from the front of the holes to the front of the rim. The standard is 450 mm. Measure the widest part of the pan at the side. The standard is 380 mm. Any seat marketed as universal will fit a pan that matches these numbers. Note whether the holes go all the way through the pan or are blind from above. Through-holes take both top-fix and bottom-fix; blind holes take only top-fix.

Push the hinge bolts down through the pan holes. Place a rubber washer on each bolt under the pan rim. Add the wing nut. Hand-tighten only at this stage. The hinge plates on top of the pan stay loose so the seat can be slid into position.
Step 4, fit the seat and align

Slide the seat onto the hinge plates until it clicks. Lower the lid. Stand back and check the seat sits central on the pan, with equal space at front and back. If the seat is offset, lift it off, loosen the wing nuts a half-turn, shift the hinge plates, and refit. Repeat until the seat sits square. Tighten the wing nuts firmly by hand. Do not over-tighten; ceramic pans crack at the rim under torque.
Step 5, snap the hinge caps closed

Press the hinge caps down over the top of the bolts until they click flat. The job is done. Lift and lower the lid a few times to check the soft-close mechanism, if the seat has one, lowers smoothly from any starting angle.

On a wall-hung pan with concealed cistern, the fixing holes may need a longer hinge bolt because the rim is thicker. A standard pack of seat bolts does not always include the longer option. Buy a seat marketed for wall-hung pans, or buy 80 mm machine screws separately and use them with the supplied rubber washers.

attrs.symptom: The old bolt spins when the wing nut is held
attrs.cause: The bolt head has rounded off, or the threads have seized in the wing nut after years of use.
attrs.fix: Cut through the bolt with a hacksaw blade between the seat hinge and the pan rim. Use the blade alone, not the saw frame, to fit in the space.

attrs.symptom: The new seat shifts side to side after a week
attrs.cause: The wing nuts loosened, or the rubber washers slipped at the back of the rim.
attrs.fix: Tighten the wing nuts by hand. If they will not stay tight, replace the supplied rubber washers with thicker neoprene washers from a plumbing-merchant counter.

attrs.symptom: The lid will not stay up by itself
attrs.cause: Cheap seats use friction hinges that wear quickly. A standard seat may also have been over-tightened, which deforms the hinge plate.
attrs.fix: Loosen the wing nuts a quarter-turn so the hinge plate sits flat. If the lid still falls, the friction hinge is worn out and the seat needs replacing again with a soft-close version.
